content:
# Luke Chapter 22 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 115-117 | **Source Images:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_022_page_001.jpg, LUK_022_page_002.jpg, LUK_022_page_003.jpg

---

**1.** And the feast of the unleavened-bread was drawing-near, the [one] being-called Pascha [Passover],

**2.** and the chief-priests and the scribes were seeking how they might destroy him, for they were fearing the people.

**3.** And Satan entered into Judas, the [one] being-called Iscariot, being from the number of the twelve;

**4.** and having gone-away he spoke-together with the chief-priests and officers [as to] how he might hand-over him to them.

**5.** And they rejoiced, and agreed to give him silver.

**6.** And he consented, and was seeking a favorable-time to hand-over him to them apart-from [a] crowd.

**7.** And the day of the unleavened-bread came, on which it was necessary [for] the Pascha [Passover lamb] to be sacrificed.

**8.** And he sent Peter and John, saying, "Having gone, prepare for us the Pascha [Passover] that we may eat [it]."

**9.** And they said to him, "Where do you will [that] we should prepare?"

**10.** And he said to them, "Behold, [when] you have entered into the city, a man carrying a jar of water will meet you; follow him into the house into which he enters,

**11.** and you will say to the master-of-the-house, 'The Teacher says to you, Where is the guest-room where I may eat the Pascha [Passover] with my disciples?'

**12.** And that [man] will show you a large upper-room having-been-furnished; there prepare."

**13.** And having gone-away they found [it] just-as he had said to them, and they prepared the Pascha [Passover].

**14.** And when the hour came, he reclined, and the apostles with him.

**15.** And he said to them, "With desire I desired to eat this Pascha [Passover] with you before my suffering;

**16.** for I say to you that I will certainly not eat of it until it should be fulfilled in the kingdom of God."

**17.** And having received a cup, having given-thanks, he said, "Take this and divide [it] among yourselves;

**18.** for I say to you that I will certainly not drink from the fruit of the vine from now until the kingdom of God should come."

**19.** And having taken bread, having given-thanks, he broke [it] and gave [it] to them, saying, "This is my body, the [one] being-given on-behalf-of you; this do in my remembrance."

**20.** And the cup likewise after the having-eaten [supper], saying, "This cup [is] the new covenant in my blood, the [one] being-poured-out on-behalf-of you.

**21.** But behold, the hand of the [one] handing-over me [is] with me upon the table.

**22.** Because the Son of Man indeed goes according-to the [thing] having been determined; but woe to that man through whom he is handed-over."

**23.** And they began to discuss among themselves [the question as to] which then of them it might be [who was] about to do this.

**24.** And there became also a contention among them, [as to] which of them seems to be greatest.

**25.** And he said to them, "The kings of the nations lord-over them, and the [ones] exercising-authority-over them are called benefactors.

**26.** But you [are] not thus; but let the greatest among you become as the younger, and the [one] leading as the [one] serving.

**27.** For who [is] greater, the [one] reclining or the [one] serving? [Is it] not the [one] reclining? But I am in the midst of you as the [one] serving.

**28.** And you are the [ones] having continued-with me in my trials;

**29.** and I appoint to you, just-as my Father appointed to me, a kingdom,

**30.** that you may eat and drink at my table in my kingdom, and you will sit upon thrones judging the twelve tribes of Israel.

**31.** Simon, Simon, behold, Satan demanded to have you [plural] to sift [you] as the wheat;

**32.** but I entreated concerning you [singular], that your faith should not fail; and you, when you have turned-back, strengthen your brothers."

**33.** And he said to him, "Lord, with you I am ready to go both into prison and into death."

**34.** And he said, "I say to you, Peter, [the] rooster will certainly not crow today until you will have denied three-times [that you] know me."

**35.** And he said to them, "When I sent you without purse and bag and sandals, did you lack anything?" And they said, "Nothing."

**36.** And he said to them, "But now, let the [one] having a purse take [it], likewise also a bag; and the [one] not having [a sword], let him sell his garment and buy a sword.

**37.** For I say to you that this, the [thing] having been written, it is necessary to be accomplished in me: 'And he was reckoned with the lawless-ones.' For indeed the [thing] concerning me has [its] end."

**38.** And they said, "Lord, behold, here [are] two swords." And he said to them, "It is enough."

**39.** And having gone-out, he went according-to [his] custom to the Mount of Olives; and the disciples also followed him.

**40.** And having come-to-be at the place, he said to them, "Pray not to enter into temptation."

**41.** And he was withdrawn from them about a stone's throw, and having placed [his] knees, he was praying,

**42.** saying, "Father, if you are willing, take-away this cup from me; but not my will, but yours let-it-be-done."

**43.** [And an angel from heaven appeared to him, strengthening him.

**44.** And having come-to-be in an agony, he was praying more earnestly; and his sweat became as-if drops of blood falling-down upon the earth.]

**45.** And having risen from the prayer, having come to the disciples, he found them sleeping from the grief,

**46.** and he said to them, "Why are you sleeping? Having risen, pray that you should not enter into temptation."

**47.** [While] he [was] yet speaking, behold, a crowd, and the [one] being-called Judas, one of the twelve, was coming-before them, and he drew-near to Jesus to kiss him.

**48.** And Jesus said to him, "Judas, with a kiss you hand-over the Son of Man?"

**49.** And those around him, having seen the [thing] about-to-be, said, "Lord, shall we strike with [the] sword?"

**50.** And a certain one of them struck the slave of the chief-priest and took-off his right ear.

**51.** And answering, Jesus said, "Permit [it] even [to] this." And having touched the ear, he healed him.

**52.** And Jesus said to those having come-upon him — chief-priests and officers of the temple and elders — "As against a robber have you come-out with swords and clubs?

**53.** [When] I was with you daily in the temple, you did not stretch-out [your] hands against me; but this is your hour and the authority of the darkness."

**54.** And having seized him, they led [him] and brought [him] into the house of the chief-priest. And Peter was following at-a-distance.

**55.** And [when] they had kindled a fire in [the] middle of the courtyard and had sat-down together, Peter was sitting in [the] midst of them.

**56.** And a certain servant-girl, having seen him sitting toward the light and having gazed-at him, said, "This [one] also was with him."

**57.** But he denied, saying, "I do not know him, woman."

**58.** And after a little [while] another, having seen him, said, "You also are of them." But Peter said, "Man, I am not."

**59.** And after about one hour had passed, a certain other [man] was insisting strongly, saying, "In truth this [one] also was with him, for he is also a Galilean."

**60.** And Peter said, "Man, I do not know what you are saying." And immediately, [while] he [was] yet speaking, [the] rooster crowed.

**61.** And having turned, the Lord looked-at Peter, and Peter remembered the word of the Lord, how he said to him, "Before [the] rooster crows today, you will deny me three-times."

**62.** And having gone-out, he wept bitterly.

**63.** And the men holding him were mocking him, beating [him],

**64.** and having covered him, they were asking him, saying, "Prophesy! Who is the [one] having struck you?"

**65.** And many other [things], blaspheming, they were saying against him.

**66.** And when it became day, the council-of-elders of the people was gathered-together, both chief-priests and scribes, and they led him away into their Sanhedrin [council], saying,

**67.** "If you are the Christ, tell us." And he said to them, "If I should tell you, you would certainly not believe;

**68.** and if I should ask, you would certainly not answer.

**69.** But from the now [on], the Son of Man will be sitting at the right [hand] of the power of God."

**70.** And they all said, "You, then, are the Son of God?" And he said to them, "You yourselves say that I am."

**71.** And they said, "What further need of testimony do we have? For we ourselves heard from his own mouth."

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript and Folio
Translated from Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), 4th century CE. The text of Luke 22 spans from the right-hand columns of PDF page 115 (folio beginning at Lk 21:24) through PDF page 117 (folio ending into Lk 23:7). The manuscript is written in continuous scriptio continua (no word separation), four columns per page, in Greek biblical uncial script.

### Critical Variant: Verses 43-44 (The Agony, Bloody Sweat, and Angelic Strengthening)
**This is the single most significant textual variant in Luke 22.** Verses 43-44 — describing an angel from heaven appearing to strengthen Jesus and his sweat becoming like drops of blood — are **present in Codex Sinaiticus** in the original hand. These verses have been bracketed in the translation to signal their disputed status. Key manuscript evidence:

- **INCLUDED:** Sinaiticus (original hand), D (Bezae), the majority of Greek manuscripts, Old Latin, Syriac Peshitta, and patristic citations (Justin Martyr, Irenaeus, Hippolytus, Epiphanius)
- **OMITTED:** p75, Vaticanus (B), Alexandrinus (A, original hand), W, T, 579, some Sahidic Coptic manuscripts, and f13
- Sinaiticus itself shows evidence of later scribal marks around these verses, but the original hand includes them
- The UBS committee rated the inclusion as {C} (considerable uncertainty), suggesting the verses may be a very early interpolation — possibly pre-2nd century — drawn from authentic tradition but not originally part of Luke's autograph
- The theological concern motivating omission may have been docetic: some early Christians objected to depicting Jesus in such extreme human anguish

**EMET's judgment:** Since the governing manuscript for this translation is Sinaiticus, and the original scribe included these verses, they are retained in the body text with brackets to indicate their contested nature. A fully honest translation must present them while flagging the dispute.

### Verse 17-20 (The Cup-Bread-Cup Sequence)
Sinaiticus preserves the "longer text" of the Last Supper, including vv. 19b-20 ("the [one] being-given on-behalf-of you; this do in my remembrance. And the cup likewise..."). This creates the distinctive Lukan cup-bread-cup sequence. Some Western witnesses (D, some Old Latin) omit vv. 19b-20, producing only a cup-bread sequence. Sinaiticus clearly includes the full text, and this translation follows Sinaiticus.

### Verse 31: "Satan demanded to have you"
The Greek exetesato (demanded / asked-for) uses a verb that implies demanding with authority, possibly a legal metaphor. The "you" (hymas) is plural — Satan's demand was for all the disciples, though Jesus' prayer in v. 32 switches to singular "you" (sou) — specifically for Peter. This plural-to-singular shift is carefully preserved in the translation.

### Verse 34: Denial Prediction
Sinaiticus reads "the rooster will certainly not crow today" (semeron), placing the temporal marker with the crowing rather than with the denial. This matches Mark 14:30 rather than Matthew 26:34's construction.

### Verse 43-44: Vocabulary Notes
- "agonia" (agony, v. 44): the only use of this word in the NT; it carries a sense of intense struggle or contest, not merely pain
- "thromboi haimatos" (drops/clots of blood, v. 44): thromboi specifically means "clots" or "thick drops," a medical term consistent with Luke's tradition as a physician-author

### Verse 51: "Permit even to this"
The Greek eate heos toutou is notoriously difficult. It could mean: (a) "Allow [things to go] as far as this" (i.e., stop, this is enough violence); (b) "Let [them do] even this" (permit the arrest to proceed); (c) "Leave off — [enough] of this!" The translation preserves the compressed ambiguity of the Greek.

### Verse 64: Sinaiticus and the Covering
Sinaiticus reads perikalypsantes auton — "having covered him." Some later manuscripts add "his face" (to prosopon autou), but Sinaiticus has only "him." The translation follows Sinaiticus precisely.

### Verse 66: "Their Sanhedrin"
The Greek to synedrion auton ("their Sanhedrin") uses the possessive pronoun, which is preserved. This may indicate Luke's distancing perspective — writing for Gentile readers for whom the Sanhedrin was a foreign institution.

### Verse 70: "You yourselves say that I am"
The Greek hymeis legete hoti ego eimi is technically an affirmation wrapped in an attribution to the questioners. Jesus neither directly claims the title nor denies it — he states that the identification comes from their own words. This delicate construction is preserved literally.

### Orthographic Notes from Sinaiticus
- Sinaiticus frequently uses nomina sacra: abbreviated forms for sacred names (e.g., IC XC for Jesus Christ, KC for Lord, THC for God, PNA for Spirit, ANOC for man/human, OYNOC for heaven)
- Itacistic spellings are common in Sinaiticus (e.g., ei/i interchange, ai/e interchange); these reflect pronunciation shifts in Koine Greek and do not affect translation
- The manuscript shows occasional scribal corrections (superlinear dots, marginal marks) throughout this chapter

---

*Translated and annotated by EMET*
*The EMET Bible — Truth Before Beauty*
*Source: Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), 4th century CE*
